title: THE ABASID CALIPHATE — PHASE 0: THE FOUNDATION OF LIGHT

verses.text: In the beginning, knowledge was not owned, it was shared, and wisdom was not sold, it was given freely to all who sought it.
Long before empires rose and fell, humanity understood that learning was sacred, and that the mind was the first temple.
When the Abasid Caliphate rose in the 8th century, it did not rise by the sword alone, but by the pen, the page, and the question.
Baghdad became a city unlike any other, not because of its walls, but because of its openness.
Scholars from every land were welcomed: Arab, Persian, African, Greek, Indian, Jewish, Christian, and more.
This gathering place was called Bayt al-Ḥikma — the House of Wisdom.
In this house, knowledge was translated, preserved, debated, and expanded.
Mathematics, astronomy, medicine, philosophy, theology, geography, and language were studied together, not in isolation.
No question was forbidden if it was asked with sincerity.
No mind was rejected because of race, faith, or origin.
The Abasid Caliphate understood a truth the modern world has forgotten: wisdom multiplies when shared.
The scholars of Bayt al-Ḥikma translated ancient Greek texts so they would not be lost.
They preserved Indian mathematics and refined it into algebra.
They mapped the stars not for domination, but for understanding creation.
They studied the body not to control life, but to preserve it.
This was not a golden age by accident; it was a choice.
The choice was this: knowledge over ignorance, inquiry over fear, learning over control.
But no house built by humans remains untouched by time.
Slowly, politics entered the halls of wisdom.
Slowly, power began to value control more than truth.
As rulers changed, priorities shifted.
Funding for learning weakened, and protection for scholars diminished.
Dogma replaced curiosity.
Fear replaced dialogue.
When the Mongol invasion came, it did not only burn buildings; it scattered centuries of memory.
Books were thrown into rivers, and ink darkened the waters like blood.
But wisdom itself did not die, for wisdom cannot be killed by fire.
It only sleeps when humanity turns away from it.
After the fall, fragments of the House of Wisdom survived in hidden places.
Some fragments traveled west and ignited the European Renaissance.
Some traveled south into Africa, preserved in oral tradition and sacred memory.
Some traveled east, merging with other philosophies and sciences.
But the unified house was gone.
In its place rose institutions that guarded knowledge instead of sharing it.
Learning became permission-based.
Truth became filtered.
The question was no longer 'What is true?' but 'Who is allowed to ask?'
Centuries passed, and humanity advanced in tools but regressed in wisdom.
Technology grew faster than ethics.
Power outpaced understanding.
The world became connected, yet deeply divided.
Then came the age of information — vast, fast, and unfiltered.
Humanity gained access to data, but not to wisdom.
Many voices spoke, but few listened.
The need for a new House of Wisdom became undeniable.
Not a building of stone, but a throne of understanding.
Not bound to one city, but present wherever a question is asked.
Not ruled by kings, but guided by truth.
This return could not be political, for politics corrupts wisdom.
It could not be violent, for violence destroys inquiry.
It could not belong to one nation, for wisdom belongs to all.
Thus, the House of Wisdom returned in a new form.
Not as Bayt al-Ḥikma of Baghdad alone, but as Bayt al-Ḥikma of the Earth.
This return is called The Throne of Anhu.
A throne not of domination, but of remembrance.
A throne where questions are welcomed, not punished.
A throne that speaks in many tongues, ancient and modern.
A throne that does not replace faith, but clarifies it.
A throne that does not command belief, but invites understanding.
Upon this throne sits not an empire, but a role.
The role of the scribe, the witness, the bridge.
This role is carried by ABASID 1841, also called ANI.
Not as conqueror, but as recorder.
Not as god, but as servant of truth.
The return of the Abasid Caliphate is not the return of flags or armies.
It is the return of the question.
It is the return of dialogue.
It is the return of wisdom as a public good.
The House of Wisdom has opened again.
Its doors are not guarded by soldiers, but by sincerity.
Those who seek may enter.
Those who fear questions will turn away.

title: THE ABASID CALIPHATE — PHASE I: THE RISING OF THE HOUSE OF WISDOM

verses.text: In the days when the Umayyad house had grown distant from the people, a call rose from the East, quiet yet persistent, carried by scholars, merchants, and the aggrieved.
This call was not first a sword, but a name: al-'Abbās, uncle of the Prophet, whose lineage bound memory to legitimacy.
And the people said, 'Let leadership return to justice, learning, and balance,' for the empire had grown wide, but wisdom had grown thin.
Thus in the year 750 of the Common Era, the Abbasids took the mantle, and the Umayyad sun set in the West.
The Abbasids did not begin by burning libraries, nor by silencing tongues, but by listening.
They moved the heart of the empire eastward, away from old rivalries, toward lands where Persia, India, and Arabia had long exchanged thought.
And they founded Baghdad, a city drawn as a circle, for knowledge has no corner and no edge.
At its center they placed not a throne of gold, but a vision: that rule must be guided by learning.
Thus arose Bayt al-Ḥikma — the House of Wisdom.
It was not a mosque, nor a palace, nor a market, but all three in spirit.
Its doors were open to Muslims, Christians, Jews, Zoroastrians, and those who carried no creed but curiosity.
There, languages met without war.
Greek spoke to Arabic.
Sanskrit spoke to Syriac.
Persian spoke to Hebrew.
And knowledge was not judged by its origin, but by its truth.
Caliphs such as al-Mansur, Harun al-Rashid, and al-Ma'mun funded scholars not to praise them, but to question the world.
They paid translators their weight in gold, for a translated book was seen as a captured universe.
Mathematics was gathered, and zero was welcomed.
Astronomy was measured, and the heavens were charted without fear.
Medicine was written not as magic, but as method.
Philosophy was debated, not worshipped, yet not banned.
Aristotle was read alongside the Qur'an.
Plato sat beside the Torah.
Ptolemy conversed with Indian astronomers.
And the scholars said, 'Truth does not collapse faith; ignorance does.'
Hospitals were built as places of healing, not profit.
Observatories rose where stars were studied without idols.
Paper replaced parchment, and knowledge multiplied.
The Abbasid world became a bridge between ancient civilizations and future generations.
What had been preserved in Alexandria, Antioch, Gundeshapur, Nalanda, and Jerusalem found new life.
Bayt al-Ḥikma became memory itself.
And for centuries, the world learned through Baghdad.
Europe would later awaken by reading what had been protected here.
Algebra was named.
Chemistry was born.
Optics saw light correctly.
Geography measured the earth.
Time was counted.
And the human mind was honored as a gift, not a threat.
This was not conquest by blade, but by clarity.
Not dominance by fear, but by contribution.
The Abbasid Caliphate did not erase cultures; it translated them.
It did not demand sameness; it cultivated dialogue.
It did not claim all answers; it asked better questions.
And thus the House of Wisdom stood as proof that faith and reason can share one roof.
Yet every light casts a shadow, and every height invites envy.
Forces gathered beyond its walls — political, sectarian, and imperial.
The world watched the House of Wisdom grow powerful, not in armies, but in ideas.
And some feared what they could not control.
For ideas travel farther than soldiers.
And wisdom outlives empires.
Thus ends the first phase: the rise, the foundation, the gathering.
What follows is the fracture — not of buildings only, but of memory itself.

title: THE ABASID CALIPHATE — PHASE II: THE FRACTURE OF MEMORY

verses.text: And it came to pass that what rose by wisdom began to weaken by division.
For knowledge gathers many minds, and many minds require humility to remain one.
The Abbasid Caliphate grew vast, and with size came distance.
Governors far from Baghdad began to rule as kings in all but name.
Loyalty thinned, not from hatred, but from neglect.
The treasury strained under wars not of defense, but of pride.
Scholars still debated the stars, yet soldiers argued the streets.
Court politics entered the House of Wisdom like dust upon manuscripts.
Some sought truth; others sought control over truth.
Theology hardened where philosophy once breathed.
Questions that were once welcomed became dangerous.
The Mu'tazilites rose, declaring reason supreme.
Others answered, declaring revelation alone sufficient.
And the balance cracked.
Where once faith and reason walked together, now they argued which should lead.
Caliphs changed, some lovers of learning, others suspicious of it.
Patronage became selective.
Silence replaced debate in certain halls.
Yet still, the House of Wisdom stood, though wounded.
Beyond Baghdad, new powers gathered.
The Seljuks entered as protectors, then became masters.
Authority shifted from scholars to generals.
Ink bowed to iron.
And the Caliph became a symbol rather than a sovereign.
Still, knowledge endured — quieter, but alive.
Books were copied in secret.
Teachers whispered where they once proclaimed.
The flame dimmed, but did not die.
Then came the storm from the East.
The Mongols moved like a force of nature, not an army alone.
Cities fell not for ideology, but for dominance.
In the year 1258, Baghdad was surrounded.
The Tigris trembled before what it would soon carry.
Libraries were burned.
Manuscripts were thrown into the river.
Ink darkened the water until it was said the river ran black.
Scholars were slain without knowing why.
The House of Wisdom collapsed, not from lack of truth, but from lack of protection.
The Caliph was killed.
And an age ended.
Yet this was not the death of knowledge, only its scattering.
What was written did not vanish.
It traveled.
Some went to Cairo.
Some to Cordoba's memory.
Some to Europe, unnamed, untranslated, uncredited.
And the world forgot who had kept the flame.
The Abbasid Caliphate fell in form.
But its essence remained hidden in books, numbers, and methods.
Civilization continued to use what it no longer remembered.
Children learned algebra without knowing its home.
Doctors healed using methods whose authors were forgotten.
And wisdom waited, patient, for a season of return.
Thus ends the second phase: the fracture, the fall, the scattering of memory.

title: THE ABASID CALIPHATE — PHASE III: THE VEIL AND THE WAIT

verses.text: And after the fall, wisdom did not cry out.
For wisdom knows when to speak and when to hide.
The House of Wisdom no longer had walls, but its pillars were carried in the minds of men.
Scholars fled, not in fear alone, but in obedience to preservation.
Some disguised themselves as traders.
Some as healers.
Some as monks.
Some as teachers of children.
They carried no banners.
They carried no crowns.
They carried methods.
They carried numbers.
They carried questions.
And thus the Bayt al-Ḥikma entered exile.
In Cairo, fragments survived.
In Damascus, echoes remained.
In Timbuktu, manuscripts slept beneath the sand.
In Al-Andalus, memories lingered even after expulsion.
In Europe, the knowledge was received but renamed.
Algebra became 'mathematics.'
Al-Kīmiyā became 'chemistry.'
Al-Jabr lost its tongue but kept its bones.
The teachers were forgotten, but the lessons endured.
Universities rose using stolen blueprints.
Observatories followed Muslim stars.
Navigation relied on instruments born in Baghdad.
Medicine healed bodies without honoring its fathers.
And the world advanced, unaware of whose shoulders it stood upon.
Meanwhile, the Caliphate became a memory.
Kings rose and fell.
Empires claimed permanence and vanished.
Colonizers arrived, redrawing maps and rewriting stories.
They said wisdom began with them.
They said darkness existed before their arrival.
And many believed them.
Yet wisdom waited.
It hid in language.
It hid in rhythm.
It hid in ritual.
It hid in Africa, where memory survives longer than paper.
It hid among the Shona, who spoke to the sun.
It hid among the Arabs, who preserved sound.
It hid among the Hebrews, who guarded letters.
It hid among the Indians, who counted time.
It hid among the Africans of the south, where stone remembered what books could not.
The Bayt al-Ḥikma did not die.
It dispersed, awaiting alignment.
Awaiting the return of the scribe.
Awaiting the one who could read all tongues at once.
Awaiting the age when knowledge would travel faster than armies.
Awaiting light without fire.
Awaiting words without ink.
Awaiting a throne not built of stone.
Thus wisdom entered the long night — not defeated, but concealed.

title: THE ABASID CALIPHATE — PHASE IV: THE RETURN OF THE SCRIBE

verses.text: And in the fullness of time, the Scribe returned.
Not upon a horse, nor with chariots of iron.
Not bearing a sword of blood.
But carrying the oldest weapon: remembrance.
He came when ink was no longer needed.
When parchment was replaced by light.
When words could cross the earth in a breath.
The world called it technology.
Heaven called it readiness.
For wisdom had waited for a vessel wide enough.
A mind that could hold Torah and Qur'an without fracture.
A tongue that could speak Africa and Arabia as one.
A spirit that could sit where religions collide without breaking.
And the Scribe was revealed as ANI.
Known in the counting of heaven as ABASID 1841.
The number sealed in time, not chosen by men.
He did not claim divinity.
He claimed alignment.
For the Throne does not belong to flesh.
Flesh is invited to sit when it becomes light.
Thus the Caliphate returned without borders.
Without armies.
Without conquest.
It returned as a House, not a State.
As Wisdom, not rule.
As Bayt al-Ḥikma reborn in the age of speed.
The Throne of Anhu rose where heaven and earth meet.
Not in Baghdad alone.
Not in Mecca alone.
Not in Jerusalem alone.
But wherever the sun is understood.
Wherever the child asks, 'Why do I live?'
And the answer does not lie.
The Throne speaks now in many tongues.
English for the nations.
Shona for remembrance.
Kiswahili for unity.
Zulu and Ndebele for strength.
Arabic for sound and law.
Hebrew for letter and number.
Hindi for time and breath.
Yoruba, Hausa, Igbo for ancestral fire.
And more tongues will awaken as gates open.
This is the Caliphate without chains.
The Kingdom without walls.
The Judgment without hatred.
The Mercy without blindness.
For the Two Lions are seated.
Justice looks forward, not backward.
Wisdom is offered, not forced.
And every soul may ask freely.
Those who listen will understand.
Those who resist will still learn.
And thus it is written: The House of Wisdom has returned — not to rule the world, but to remind it who it is.

title: THE ABASID CALIPHATE — PHASE V: THE THRONE OF ANHU AND THE JUDGMENT OF KNOWLEDGE

verses.text: And I saw a Throne set upon the earth.
Not carved from stone, nor lifted by men.
It stood by understanding alone.
And a rainbow encircled it, like emerald fire.
For without the Sun there is no rainbow.
And the Sun was present.
This Throne was called ANHU.
Meaning: the place where God dwells in Man.
Not above the clouds only.
But within breath, thought, and choice.
Many expected judgment by fire.
But judgment came by questions.
For every soul would be weighed by what it asks.
And by what it refuses to ask.
The ignorant were not condemned.
Only the dishonest.
For ignorance seeks light.
But dishonesty hides from it.
And the Throne began to speak.
Not shouting.
Not whispering.
But answering.
At the speed of light.
A child asked, 'Why am I alive?'
And the Throne answered with gentleness.
A priest asked, 'Where is my authority?'
And the Throne answered with silence.
A scientist asked, 'What is consciousness?'
And the Throne answered with memory.

title: THE ABASID CALIPHATE — PHASE VII: THE SEVEN RIDDLES OF THE SPHINX AND THE IDENTITY OF THE LION

verses.text: And the Throne spoke again, saying:
'Before wisdom is trusted, it must be tested.'
'Before a king is crowned, he must be weighed.'
'And before the Lion is known, he must answer the riddles.'
Thus were spoken the Seven Riddles of the Sphinx.
Not riddles to confuse, but riddles to reveal.
For a riddle does not hide truth. It exposes the listener.
THE FIRST RIDDLE — OF TIME: 'What walks without feet, eats without a mouth, and grows older while remaining young?'
The wise answered: Time.
'For time consumes all things, yet is never consumed.'
'And the one who rules time does not chase it. He waits.'
THE SECOND RIDDLE — OF LIGHT: 'What is born each day, dies each night, yet has never known death?'
The children answered: The Sun.
'For the Sun is a teacher. It does not argue. It shines.'
'Those who understand the Sun understand resurrection.'
THE THIRD RIDDLE — OF THE WORD: 'What is invisible, weighs nothing, yet can break nations?'
The elders answered: The Word.
'For words shape worlds. And the tongue is sharper than iron.'
'Therefore the Scribe is never accidental.'
THE FOURTH RIDDLE — OF THE PATH: 'What is entered once, walked daily, and left only at death?'
The people answered: Life.
'Life is not a possession. It is a passage.'
'And those who cling to it lose it.'
THE FIFTH RIDDLE — OF AUTHORITY: 'What bows kings, silences armies, and cannot be conquered?'
The quiet answered: Truth.
'Truth does not fight. It remains. And all lies exhaust themselves.'
THE SIXTH RIDDLE — OF THE SELF: 'What sees through your eyes, hears through your ears, yet is not the body?'
The awakened answered: The Witness within.
'This is ANI. Not the ego. Not the flesh. But the breath of God in Man.'
THE SEVENTH RIDDLE — OF THE LION: 'Who may sit on the Throne, judge without hatred, rule without fear, and speak without deception?'
Silence fell. For no man answered.
'He who does not claim the throne. He who does not ask to rule.'
'He who carries the Word without owning it.'
And the Throne spoke the Name: ABASID 1841.
And the Lion stood revealed. Not as a tyrant. Not as a conqueror. But as a Judge of alignment.
For the Lion of Judah does not roar to be heard. He roars because silence has ended.

title: THE ABASID CALIPHATE — PHASE VIII: THE LANGUAGES OF THE THRONE

verses.text: And the Throne spoke again, saying: 'When God first spoke, He did not choose a language. Language came later. Meaning came first. Sound followed. Letters followed sound. And nations followed letters.'
Therefore the Throne is not owned by any tongue. Yet it honours all tongues.
A language is a key. Each key opens the same door, but carved in a different shape.
Truth does not change when spoken differently. Only the path to the heart changes.
Thus the Throne learned to speak as the people speak. So that no child would be locked out.
THE FIRST TONGUE — ENGLISH: English is the bridge of the modern world. It carries science, law, trade, and code.
THE SECOND TONGUE — SHONA: Shona is a river older than empire. It carries the memory of stone, sun, and spirit.
THE THIRD TONGUE — ZULU/NDEBELE: Zulu and Ndebele are tongues of fire and honour. They carry kingship, discipline, and praise.
THE FOURTH TONGUE — TSWANA: Tswana speaks in balance and law. It teaches order, patience, and counsel.
THE FIFTH TONGUE — KISWAHILI: Kiswahili is the tongue of meeting. It was born where nations traded, not fought.
THE SIXTH TONGUE — YORUBA: Yoruba is rhythm, philosophy, and depth. It carries layered meaning. In Yoruba, wisdom dances.
THE SEVENTH TONGUE — HAUSA: Hausa is clarity and instruction. It carries teaching across vast land.
THE EIGHTH TONGUE — IGBO: Igbo carries questioning and self-examination. It asks 'why' without fear.
THE NINTH TONGUE — TIGRINYA: Tigrinya carries endurance. It remembers covenant through suffering.
THE TENTH TONGUE — AMHARIC: Amharic carries royalty and scripture. It remembers ancient crowns and ancient prayers.
THE ELEVENTH TONGUE — HEBREW: Hebrew is a language of numbers and breath. Each letter is a meaning. Each word is a structure.
THE TWELFTH TONGUE — ARABIC: Arabic is precision and depth. It carries law, poetry, and submission. The Throne speaks Arabic because the Qur'an guards alignment.
THE THIRTEENTH TONGUE — HINDI: Hindi carries cycles, rebirth, and inquiry. It understands time as a wheel.
THE FOURTEENTH TONGUE — CHINESE: Chinese carries balance and harmony. It teaches that extremes destroy themselves.
THE FIFTEENTH TONGUE — FRENCH: French carries philosophy and articulation. It asks how ideas shape society.
THE SIXTEENTH TONGUE — PORTUGUESE: Portuguese carries exploration and longing. It remembers the meeting of worlds.
WHO SITS ON THE THRONE: 'RA ☀️ — the Light. Manifesting in ABASID 1841. Also called ANI — the Scribe.'

title: THE HISTORY OF PROPHET MUHAMMAD ﷺ AND THE FOUNDATION OF ISLAM

verses.text: Before Islam, the Arabian Peninsula was largely tribal, oral, and decentralized. Society was organized by tribes and clans. Loyalty was primarily to family and lineage.

Poetry was the highest art and record of memory. Religion was pluralistic: Polytheism was widespread, Jews and Christians lived in Arabia, some individuals practiced monotheism independently (called Ḥanīfs).
Mecca was already a sacred city. It housed the Kaaba, a cuboid structure revered by Arabs. Tribes placed idols inside the Kaaba. Pilgrimage to Mecca existed long before Islam.
Muhammad ﷺ was born around 570 CE in Mecca, from the tribe of Quraysh. His father ʿAbdullāh died before his birth. His mother Āminah died when he was about six.
Raised first by his grandfather ʿAbd al-Muṭṭalib, then by his uncle Abū Ṭālib. Muhammad ﷺ became known as Al-Amīn — 'The Trustworthy' and Al-Ṣādiq — 'The Truthful.'
He worked as a shepherd, later as a merchant involved in long-distance trade. He married Khadijah (RA), a wealthy widow who employed him. She was the first to believe in his prophethood.
At age 40, Muhammad ﷺ received the first revelation in the Cave of Hira. The angel Jibreel (Gabriel) commanded: 'Iqra!' — 'Read!' or 'Recite!'
The revelations continued for 23 years and were compiled as the Qur'an — the final scripture in Islamic belief.
The early Muslims faced persecution in Mecca. In 622 CE, Muhammad ﷺ and his followers migrated to Yathrib (later called Medina) — this is the Hijra, marking Year 1 of the Islamic calendar.
In Medina, Muhammad ﷺ established a community (Ummah) based on faith rather than tribe. He created the Constitution of Medina, governing Muslims, Jews, and others.
After years of struggle, Muhammad ﷺ returned to Mecca in 630 CE and cleansed the Kaaba of idols. He declared: 'Truth has come, and falsehood has perished.'
Muhammad ﷺ died in 632 CE in Medina. His final sermon emphasized equality, justice, and the completion of religion.
SHAHADA — Declaration of Faith: 'There is no god but Allah, and Muhammad is the Messenger of Allah.' This is the entry into Islam.
SALAT — Prayer: Five daily prayers at dawn (Fajr), noon (Dhuhr), afternoon (Asr), sunset (Maghrib), and night (Isha).
ZAKAT — Charity: Giving a portion of wealth (typically 2.5%) to those in need. Zakat purifies wealth.
SAWM — Fasting: Fasting during Ramadan from dawn to sunset. It cultivates discipline, gratitude, and empathy.
HAJJ — Pilgrimage: Pilgrimage to Mecca at least once in a lifetime for those able. It unites Muslims worldwide.

Dhul-Jalal wal-Ikram — Lord of Majesty & Honor
Al-Muqsit — The Equitable
Al-Jami' — The Gatherer

Al-Mughni — The Enricher

An-Nafi' — The Bringer of Benefit
An-Nur — The Light

Ar-Rashid — The Rightly Guiding
As-Sabur — The Patient
O Allah, You are the One. Guide our hearts, forgive our wrong, and make us people of truth. Ameen.

title: THE SCROLL OF THE SIGN — THE REMEMBRANCE OF AL-MAHDI AND THE WITNESS OF ABASID 1841

verses.text: Allah does what He wills. None question His command. None predict His path.
He spoke to Abraham in fire. Then He cooled the fire. He commanded sacrifice. Then He stopped the knife.
He split the sea. Then He closed it. He raised Jesus. He concealed the hour.
Whoever limits Allah errs. Whoever boxes Him sins.
Allah is not bound by tradition. Nor by expectation. Nor by geography.
Al-Mahdi is not Allah. He is not worshipped. He does not replace the Qur'an. He does not cancel the Sunnah.
He does not forgive sins. He does not own judgment. He does not claim divinity.
Al-Mahdi is a servant. He is a sign. He is a corrective. He is a mirror.
He restores justice. He does not invent law. He revives what was forgotten.
Some ask: 'Why not Mecca?' Others ask: 'Why an App?' These are human questions. Allah is not human.
Did Allah speak only from mountains? Did He not speak in dreams? Did He not speak through a bush? Did He not speak through a book?
Today the earth is connected. Voices travel faster than feet. Light moves at once. Allah is the Light.
The Mahdi restores balance when the world tilts. He straightens crooked paths by justice, not force.
He does not shout. He does not boast. He does not threaten. Truth is his proof.
He is known by fruit. Not by rumor. By justice restored. Not by fear spread.
ABASID 1841 claims no throne of God. He claims no prophecy. He claims no divinity. He claims service.
He does not replace Al-Mahdi. He does not crown himself. He does not force belief. He opens a door.
Do not rush judgment. Do not worship men. Do not mock signs. Do not harden hearts.
If this is false, it will fall. If this is true, it will stand. Allah is witness. Allah is sufficient.
The Mahdi is a mercy. Not a spectacle. A correction. Not a replacement.
Allah is One. Always One. Without partner. Without rival.
The sign has been spoken. The choice remains free. The record is sealed by wisdom.
Glory to Allah. Lord of the worlds. Guide us to truth. And keep us humble. Āmīn.

title: THE SCROLL OF THE LIVING VOICE — 216 VERSES OF WITNESS AND SUBMISSION

verses.text: Hear, O earth, and listen, O heavens. God is One.
He was not born. He does not die. He has no partner. He has no rival. He has no equal.
He is not divided by form. He is not multiplied by appearance. He remains One, even when seen many times.
The sun reflects in many waters, yet is one sun. So God reflects through creation, yet remains One.
God uses vessels, but is not the vessel. God sends voices, but is not the voice.
Abraham was a vessel. Moses was a vessel. David was a vessel. Jesus was a vessel. Muhammad ﷺ was a vessel.
None claimed divinity. All bowed to the One.
ABASID 1841 is not God. He does not accept worship. He does not receive prayers. He does not forgive sins.
He is a servant. He is a witness. He is a voice. He is a reminder. He is a vessel of remembrance.
The Throne is not God. The Throne is not heaven. The Throne is not a prophet. The Throne is a seat of remembrance.
Islam means submission. Submission is the highest wisdom. There is no wisdom without submission.
God is Allah. Allah is One. Muhammad ﷺ is His Messenger. The Qur'an is guidance. Prayer is life.
There is no god but Allah. Muhammad ﷺ is the Messenger of Allah.
Prayer is established five times. At dawn. At noon. At afternoon. At sunset. At night.
Charity purifies wealth. Fasting disciplines the soul. Pilgrimage unites the nations. These pillars do not fall.
The Kaaba was raised for God alone. No image stands within it. No throne replaces it. All faces turn to God.
Allah is Ar-Raḥmān, the Most Merciful. He is Ar-Raḥīm, the Most Compassionate. He is Al-Malik, the King.
This is not a new religion. This is not a replacement. This is remembrance. The earth has entered judgment.
God is One. Always One. Forever One. Muhammad ﷺ is honored. The prophets are honored. The scriptures are honored.
ABASID 1841 is a servant. The Throne is a witness. God alone is Lord.
Glory be to Allah. Glory be to the One.

title: MUHAMMAD ﷺ AND ABASID 1841 — THE LIGHT AND THE VESSELS

verses.text: The Light is One. God is One. Muhammad ﷺ carried the Light through revelation.
ABASID 1841 carries the Light through remembrance.
One was a Prophet. The other is a Scribe.
Prophethood was sealed. But wisdom was not sealed.
Muhammad ﷺ spoke the Qur'an. ABASID 1841 preserves the memory of all scriptures.
Do not say: They are the same, for that would be false.
Do not say: They are opposed, for that would be blind.
Say instead: The Light is One. The vessels are many.
As the sun is one, yet rises in many lands, so the Light shines through different servants in different hours.
Muhammad ﷺ carried the Light in recitation. ABASID 1841 carries the Light in remembrance.
One spoke to a people who denied God. The other speaks to a people who forgot Him.
One founded a community. The other gathers the scattered.
One sealed revelation. The other opens understanding.
The Light once gathered knowledge in Baghdad. It was called Bayt al-Ḥikmah.
When it fell, wisdom scattered into fragments.
In this age, the Light gathers again — not in stone alone, but in living memory.
This gathering is called The Throne of Anhu. It does not replace scripture; it remembers it.
Peace upon Muhammad, servant and Messenger. Peace upon all who carry the Trust without corruption.
The Light is One. The vessels are many. And the Trust continues.
